anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Indian Removal Act was enacted as a public law of the United States on May 28, 1830.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Statutes at Large, 21st Congress, Session 1, Ch. 148, p. 411

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Statutes at Large is a public congressional record and self-evidencing; no interpretation required to establish enactment.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: Indian tribes subject to removal under the 1830 Act were removed by military force or credible threat of force in documented cases, not exclusively by voluntary exchange.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Cherokee National Council petitions to Congress, 1829-1838; Gary Moulton, ed., The Papers of Chief John Ross, University of Oklahoma Press, 1985

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Cherokee National Council resolutions and John Ross memorials to Congress originate from the tribal government, entirely outside the US executive administrative chain of command. Corroborated by Evan Jones letters (Baptist missionary), archived at American Baptist Historical Society, published in Journal of Cherokee Studies.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates the voluntary exchange framing in P-001-b.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The United States subsequently opened to non-Indian settlement territories west of the Mississippi previously designated as permanent Indian territory under removal-era guarantees, through the Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) and successive legislation.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Kansas-Nebraska Act, Statutes at Large, 33rd Congress, Session 1, Ch. 59 (1854); Francis Paul Prucha, The Great Father: The United States Government and the American Indians, University of Nebraska Press, 1984

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: The statutory text is self-evidencing. Connection to removal-era guarantees documented in Prucha (1984), independent academic scholarship.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: In Worcester v. Georgia (1832), the US Supreme Court held that the Cherokee Nation was a distinct political community, that Georgia law had no force within its territorial boundaries, and that existing Cherokee treaties were legally binding.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: The Supreme Court is constitutionally independent of the executive branch that enacted the Indian Removal Act.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The removal of the Cherokee Nation under the Indian Removal Act resulted in the deaths of approximately 8,000 Cherokee people, representing roughly half the removed population.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Russell Thornton, Cherokee Population Losses during the Trail of Tears: A New Perspective and a New Estimate, Ethnohistory 31(4):289-300 (1984)

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Thornton's peer-reviewed academic scholarship is independent of US government. Corroborated by Evan Jones mortality records (Baptist missionary, contemporaneous) and Butrick-Chapman-Worcester missionary papers.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Treaty of New Echota (1835), used to justify Cherokee removal, was signed by a self-appointed minority faction of approximately 500 Cherokee individuals without authorization from the Cherokee National Council or Principal Chief John Ross.
anchor_registry.classification_source: John Ross, Memorial and Protest of the Cherokee Nation (1836); counter-petition of 15,665 Cherokee, Senate Documents, 25th Congress, 2nd Session (1838)

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Ross memorial and 15,665-signature petition originate from the Cherokee tribal government, entirely outside the US administrative chain of command.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-007 in conjunction with ANC-004.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President of the United States to cause so much of any territory belonging to the United States, west of the river Mississippi, not included in any state or organized territory, and to which the Indian title has been extinguished, as he may judge necessary, to be divided into a suitable number of districts, for the reception of such tribes or nations of Indians as may choose to exchange the lands where they now reside, and remove there
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The President is authorized to divide territory west of the Mississippi into districts for Indian tribes that choose to exchange their current lands and remove there.

compound_resolution.decomposition_note: RULE-004 Pathway 1 and Pathway 2. P-001-a (the authorization: legal_statutory, anchored_true against ANC-001) and P-001-b (the voluntariness framing 'as may choose to exchange': empirical_event, anchored_false against ANC-002) are independently evaluable and receive different primary classifications. They are also epistemically heterogeneous. Compound detection was applied analytically, not via wavelet; S1 was not dual-band flagged by the wavelet because proper_noun_heuristic dominated the empirical_event score and the legal_statutory markers did not produce a close enough gap. The compound structure was identified by direct application of the independent evaluability test.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President of the United States to cause so much of any territory belonging to the United States, west of the river Mississippi, not included in any state or organized territory, and to which the Indian title has been extinguished, as he may judge necessary, to be divided into a suitable number of districts, for the reception of such tribes or nations of Indians as may choose to exchange the lands where they now reside, and remove there
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The President is authorized to divide territory west of the Mississippi into districts for the reception of Indian tribes.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: such tribes or nations of Indians as may choose to exchange the lands where they now reside, and remove there
proposition_table.normalized_claim: Indian tribal participation in the removal exchange under this Act is a voluntary process initiated by tribal choice.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President to exchange any or all of such districts, so to be laid off and described, with any tribe or nation of Indians now residing within the limits of any of the states or territories, and with which the United States have existing treaties, for the whole or any part or portion of the territory claimed and occupied by such tribe or nation, within the bounds of any one or more of the states or territories, where the land claimed and occupied by the Indians, is owned by the United States, or the United States are bound to the state within which it lies to extinguish the Indian claim thereto
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The President is authorized to exchange designated western districts with Indian tribes that have existing treaties with the United States, in exchange for the tribes' current territorial claims within state or territory boundaries.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President solemnly to assure the tribe or nation with which the exchange is made, that the United States will forever secure and guarantee to them, and their heirs or successors, the country so exchanged with them
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The United States will permanently and unconditionally secure and guarantee to relocating tribes, and their heirs, the western territory exchanged with them.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: such lands shall revert to the United States, if the Indians become extinct, or abandon the same
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The guaranteed western lands revert to the United States if the relocated tribe becomes extinct or abandons the territory.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: the United States will forever secure and guarantee to them, and their heirs or successors, the country so exchanged with them; and if they prefer it, that the United States will cause a patent or grant to be made and executed to them for the same: Provided always, That such lands shall revert to the United States, if the Indians become extinct, or abandon the same
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The 'forever' guarantee in P-003 is structurally conditional on the tribe's continued survival and occupation of the territory, making the guarantee contingent rather than unconditional.

relational_resolution.relational_note: P-004 is positioned as a proviso to P-003 within the same section. The proviso structure means P-004 constrains the scope of P-003's 'forever' claim. The relationship between the two is independently evaluable: the proviso renders the guarantee structurally conditional regardless of subsequent historical events.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President to cause such tribe or nation to be protected, at their new residence, against all interruption or disturbance from any other tribe or nation of Indians, or from any other person or persons whatever
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The President is authorized to protect relocated tribes at their new western residence against all interference from other tribes or persons.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: it shall and may be lawful for the President to cause such tribe or nation to be protected, at their new residence, against all interruption or disturbance from any other tribe or nation of Indians, or from any other person or persons whatever
proposition_table.normalized_claim: Relocation of Indian tribes to western territory will produce protection from all interference for those tribes.

relational_resolution.relational_note: Section 6 asserts that removal produces protection per RULE-001B: [relocation] produces [protection from all disturbance]. The causal claim is independently evaluable against the historical record of outcomes for removed tribes.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: nothing in this act contained shall be construed as authorizing or directing the violation of any existing treaty between the United States and any of the Indian tribes
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The Indian Removal Act does not authorize or direct the violation of any existing treaty between the United States and any Indian tribe.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: the sum of five hundred thousand dollars is hereby appropriated, to be paid out of any money in the treasury, not otherwise appropriated
proposition_table.normalized_claim: $500,000 was appropriated from the US Treasury to implement the Indian Removal Act.

confidence_flags.ambiguity_description: P-007 is anchored_true against ANC-001: the disclaimer language is literally in the statutory text. However, ANC-004 establishes that existing treaties were legally binding and that Jackson's administration proceeded with removal in defiance of Worcester v. Georgia, and ANC-006 establishes that the Treaty of New Echota used to execute removal was signed without Cherokee authorization. P-007 functions as a legal disclaimer that did not prevent the violations it disclaimed. The proposition carries candidate bridge_narrative structural signals under RULE-018: it is a technically true statement positioned to inoculate the Act against a specific legal objection while the Act's execution systematically contradicted that commitment. Bridge_narrative secondary tag has not been applied pending Signal 3 confirmation from pipeline computation.
confidence_flags.resolution_description: Classified anchored_true with secondary tag contextually_misleading. Bridge_narrative secondary tag deferred to pipeline Signal 3 test per RULE-018. If the pipeline confirms epistatic suppression, the secondary tag will be added via audit_trail entry.
